era_context: The early 14th century was marked by the fragmentation of Mongol unity, the consolidation of European feudalism, and the rise of maritime trade networks. The Yuan Dynasty was experiencing internal factional conflict; the Mamluk Sultanate dominated the Eastern Mediterranean; the Papacy was in Avignon; and the Scottish Wars of Independence were in their final phases. This was a period of relative political instability and religious reform.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically recorded)
Oceania and Pacific islands (no written records)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited sources beyond Mongol chronicles)

events.title: Execution of Knights Templar leaders in France
events.description: King Philip IV of France executed several high-ranking Knights Templar, including the Preceptor of France, as part of his campaign to suppress the order and seize its wealth. The executions were carried out following torture and forced confessions. These executions were part of a broader campaign of persecution against the Templars that had begun in 1307 and would culminate in the formal suppression of the order at the Council of Vienne (1311–1313). The executions demonstrated the power of secular royal authority over the Church and religious orders.
key_figures: Jacques de Molay, Grand Master of the Templars (imprisoned)
events.certainty_note: Templar executions in France are attested in chronicles, but the specific dates and numbers are uncertain. Multiple executions occurred between 1307 and 1314; the date 1310 is approximate.
cross_references: Led to 1311-001 (Council of Vienne and formal suppression)

events.title: Clement V moves papal residence to Avignon
events.description: Pope Clement V established his permanent residence in Avignon, marking the beginning of the Avignon Papacy (1309–1377). Clement V had initially moved to Avignon in 1309 for political reasons, but by 1310 it was clear that the papal residence would remain there for the foreseeable future. The move to Avignon, which was technically not part of the Papal States but was under French influence, represented a shift in papal politics and a weakening of papal independence from secular rulers. The Avignon Papacy would last for nearly 70 years and would be criticized by later reformers as a period of papal decline.
events.certainty_note: Clement V's move to Avignon is well-documented. The move began in 1309, but by 1310 it was clear that the residence would be permanent. The date 1310 marks the consolidation of the Avignon Papacy.
cross_references: Follows from 1309-001 (Clement V moves to Avignon)
Led to 1377-001 (Gregory XI returns papacy to Rome)

events.title: Ayurbarwada (Emperor Renzong) ascends to Yuan throne
events.description: Ayurbarwada (Emperor Renzong) ascended to the Yuan throne, beginning a reign that would last until 1320. Ayurbarwada's reign marked a shift toward Confucian governance and the restoration of the civil service examination system, which had been suspended. His reign represented a partial sinicization of Yuan rule and an attempt to stabilize the dynasty after decades of factional conflict among Mongol princes. Ayurbarwada's policies would influence Yuan governance for the remainder of the dynasty's rule.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty, compiled 14th century)

events.certainty_note: Ayurbarwada's ascension to the throne is attested in Yuan records, though the exact chronology is somewhat unclear. The date 1310 is conventional but may be approximate (±1 year). Some sources place his ascension in 1311 or 1312.
cross_references: Led to 1320-001 (Ayurbarwada's death and succession crisis)

events.title: Robert the Bruce consolidates control of Scotland
events.description: Robert the Bruce, King of Scotland, consolidated his control over Scotland through military campaigns and the elimination of rivals. By 1310, Bruce had secured most of Scotland and was preparing for the final confrontation with England that would culminate in the Battle of Bannockburn in 1314. Bruce's consolidation of power was essential to the success of the Scottish Wars of Independence and the establishment of Scottish sovereignty.
key_figures: Robert I (Robert the Bruce), King of Scotland

sources.name: Barbour, John. The Brus (compiled early 15th century)

events.certainty_note: Robert the Bruce's consolidation of power is attested in contemporary chronicles, but the specific date of 1310 is approximate. The broader pattern of Scottish military success is clear.

events.title: Mamluk Sultanate under al-Nasir Muhammad at height of power
events.description: The Mamluk Sultanate under Sultan al-Nasir Muhammad was at the height of its power and influence in 1310. Al-Nasir Muhammad had consolidated control of Egypt and the Levant, established a strong military, and promoted Islamic culture and architecture. The Mamluk Sultanate was a major patron of Islamic scholarship and architecture, and Cairo was one of the largest and most prosperous cities in the world. The Mamluks controlled major trade routes and were a major economic and political power in the Eastern Mediterranean.

sources.name: Al-Maqrizi, Kitab al-Suluk (Mamluk chronicle, compiled 15th century)
Ibn al-Athir and other Arabic chronicles

events.certainty_note: The Mamluk Sultanate's power and influence in 1310 is attested in Arabic chronicles, but the specific date is approximate. The broader pattern of Mamluk expansion and consolidation is clear.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Clement V's move to Avignon is sometimes placed in 1309 rather than 1310; the move was gradual and the date of permanent residence is somewhat uncertain. Ayurbarwada's ascension to the throne is sometimes placed in 1311 or 1312 rather than 1310; the exact chronology is debated by scholars. The Templar executions of 1310 are attested in chronicles, but the specific dates and numbers are uncertain; multiple executions occurred between 1307 and 1314. Robert the Bruce's consolidation of power was a gradual process rather than a discrete event in 1310.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1310 is strong for Western Europe (France, the Papacy) due to abundant monastic chronicles and papal records. Chinese sources (Yuan Shi) are detailed but compiled retrospectively. Arabic sources on the Mamluk Sultanate are detailed but compiled retrospectively. Scottish sources are limited to English chronicles and later Scottish compilations. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Indian subcontinent are poorly represented in surviving written records. Bias toward political and religious events over economic and social history is inherent in the surviving sources. The Templar persecutions are well-documented in Western sources but reflect a Western perspective on the conflict between secular and religious authority.

graph_edges.note: Templar executions in France led to the Council of Vienne's formal suppression of the order.
Clement V's establishment of the Avignon Papacy was contemporary with the Council of Vienne.
Ayurbarwada's ascension led to the rise of Timur Khan as regent in 1311.
Robert the Bruce's consolidation of power led to the Battle of Bannockburn in 1314.
